original_abstract:
Rhythm resides in the mind: Reflections on rhythm in natural language

This article explores contemporary theories regarding the nature of rhythm in natural languages. Traditionally, languages are categorized into three rhythmic types based on the perception of stress, syllable, or mora. In stress-timed languages, accented syllables are thought to occur at regular intervals; conversely, in syllable-timed and mora-timed languages, it is the syllables or moras that appear to follow an equal temporal distribution. Although extensive phonetic research has failed to provide empirical evidence for physical isochrony in the speech stream, a wealth of data suggests that rhythm remains a fundamental component of language processing. This paper argues that phonetic models treating rhythm as a mere secondary phenomenon are insufficient for describing diverse linguistic systems. Ultimately, we demonstrate that the intuitive concept of rhythm is an essential element that must be integrated into formal phonological analysis.
humanized_abstract:
Rhythm resides in the mind: Reflections on rhythm in natural language

Theories of rhythm in natural languages, even in recent time, still remain as a topic of discussion in the field of linguistics. Common assumption in linguistics is that all the natural languages in the world can be grouped into three different rhythmic types based upon the principle of stress, syllable or mora. As we know the stress timed languages have their accented syllables at equal interval of time. Similarly in syllable-timed or mora-timed languages, syllables or moras come at equal intervals of time in speech. Though there has not been any phonetic evidence to show physical isochrony in the speech product, yet huge number of data is available to establish the importance of rhythm as a component in speech processes. The discussion on different theories is carried out showing that as the phonetic models based upon considering the rhythm as an accessory have failed to explain the totality of the structures of all the types of language, therefore it becomes an essential task to include this concept in the formal theories of phonology.

document.input:
Rhythm resides in the mind: Reflections on rhythm in natural language

This article explores contemporary theories regarding the nature of rhythm in natural languages. Traditionally, languages are categorized into three rhythmic types based on the perception of stress, syllable, or mora. In stress-timed languages, accented syllables are thought to occur at regular intervals; conversely, in syllable-timed and mora-timed languages, it is the syllables or moras that appear to follow an equal temporal distribution. Although extensive phonetic research has failed to provide empirical evidence for physical isochrony in the speech stream, a wealth of data suggests that rhythm remains a fundamental component of language processing.
